sentences.text: No.
No.
I just went to the office.
And same day, I got a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't-- I probably-- I must-- yeah, I had a passport.
The first time, when I went to Italy, I had to have a passport.
So I did have a passport.
So I just went to-- I think just took my passport and went to the-- to the office.
And the officer just stamped my passport and wished me good luck.
Was it expensive to get a visa?
Was it expensive to get a visa to Palestine?
No.
Expensive?
Didn't cost anything.
But it was difficult.
The British-- the British government stopped the Jewish immigration afterwards, altogether, because of the conflict with the Arabs.
By boat from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Trieste, in Italy.
It took about four days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was one thing-- I don't know how to say it, how to tell it.
It's kind of delicate, risque.
[LAUGHS] I was ordered for a physical for the army.
You know, I had to go, like every other citizen, I had to go for a physical.
And so that was-- I don't remember when that-- what that was.
But it was already during the Hitler regime, the beginning, I think.
And there were a lot of young men, you know, my age or so.
And the whole group-- you know, and then we were-- each one was called.
There was-- there was a row of-- there was a row of officers, military officers sitting there at a table, a long table.
And there, in the middle, stood the doctor who examined.
And-- and I was cold.
And we were all naked, you know.
And-- and then, first, he made an approving face.
You know, because I was in good shape.
It was after the-- after I had worked in the forest, so I was in good physical shape.
And many of the others were-- were from-- from poor classes.
You know, they were undernourished.
And so he was-- he looked approvingly, you know.
Good-- good-- good cannon fodder.
And-- and then he looked up, and he looked down to my penis.
And you know, at that time, only Jews were circumcised.
So he asked me, what did you have there?
You know, as if it were an operation for some sexual disease, or something.
So I didn't know what he was-- what he was looking at.
What he was--
And at that moment, one of the officers from the table there, called, this man is a Jew.
[LAUGHS]
So that was the end of-- and then, yeah, then they called me to the table.
And said that I could-- that I could volunteer for the army, for army service.
So I declined, thankfully.
And this was for military service in Germany?
Yeah, right.
So I could still, even as a Jew, I could have-- I could have served.
What happened after that?
Well, then I left.
I don't remember exactly when that was.
If it was between the time that I worked in the forest in Italy-- probably at that time.
Where?
First, I lived in a kibbutz.
And I worked-- started to work in orange groves.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was very hard labor-- very very, very, very hard.
We had big-- big hoes like this, with a short handle.
And we had to-- to loosen the earth around the trees [INAUDIBLE], so that the water would sink in.
And these orange groves were-- were owned by-- by individual owners.
And they were very tough.
They were used to Arab work.
And they-- they paid them just a little.
And-- and they had to work like slaves, you know.
So the Jews wanted to have these jobs, you know.
And so we had to-- we had to work like-- like slaves there.
I had to make a-- some-- a certain number of these trees, you know, during the day.
If you didn't, they says, don't come tomorrow.
You know.
So at first, the first day, I-- I almost fainted.
It was so hot in August.
You know, it was a-- it was a young orange grove.
The trees were small.
And the sun-- there was no protection from the sun.
So I almost fainted there.
And next to us was standing a guard with a rifle.
You know, because this was the beginning of the-- of the uprising, of the Arab uprising.
And in the morning, we drove-- we were driven by big trucks from our kibbutz to the various orange groves.
With steel plates-- they put steel plates on the trucks to protect us from shooting, from bullets.
And-- and then, that kibbutz was always under fire from the Arab side.
Too religious, especially-- today they are towns.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Sometimes they appear in the newspapers.
And then because I was just a good worker, they sent me to road-- to a road gang, you know, to build roads.
And-- but that was not like today, you had big machinery that do the work.
We had to-- we had to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] trucks came, and brought big rocks, you know, from the sea, mostly.
So with big sledge hammers, we-- we cut these rocks into smaller pieces.
And then, to make gravel, we cut-- everything by hand, and everything on our knees, you know, and in the heat.
And-- and then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we worked piece work.
You had to make so-- so many foot of [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] rocks, you know, layered rocks.
And then afterwards, they put tar on it, you know, and made the road.
And so we got paid by how much we made.
And I was so-- and others just threw the rocks, you know, threw them whichever way they could.
You know-- and I had to fit every rock to the other.
It was typical German, you know, the-- the-- accurate-- the accuracy in which we do everything.
Everything just so, just correct.
And in that situation, you couldn't do that.
And until finally, I couldn't keep up with them.
After four weeks, I almost had a breakdown, a nervous breakdown.
So they sent me home.
And so from then, they sent me to the port in Tel Aviv.
There was no port in Tel Aviv.
But the Arabs had closed the Port of Haifa to Jewish shipping.
So the Jews started to build a port.
And there were no natural facilities for a port.
So first, they built a-- they built a wooden pier.
And wood-- and wood went to the ships and took the cargo from the ships on the boat, and then rowed to the shore.
And then we came and took from the boats, you know, everything.
And carried it to shore.
So that's where they sent me, because I was good at carrying already.
Because I had learned it in Italy, to carry heavy sacks, heavy bags and sacks.
So I that's-- I was happy there, because it was-- it was the sea there.
There was a breeze in the morning.
And sometimes we sit and waited for a ship, you know, sat in the seat and rested.
It was nice.
But then I couldn't continue.
After a few months, I couldn't-- because of my shoulder.
You know, when I carried things, you know, and pushed my shoulder-- a number of times, pushed my shoulder, and they had to bring me to first-aid to set it back.
You know, and so I couldn't continue there.
And then they sent me back to the kibbutz.
And I didn't like it there.
So I left.
At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, there was a Jewish community in the country.
There was a semi-official Jewish government.
The actual government was Britain.
They governed Palestine on a mandate from the-- at that time it was called the League of Nations.
So the former Ottoman Empire, Palestine was a part of the former Ottoman Empire, as was almost all of North Africa and Asia, and this part of Asia, from Turkey all the way down.
And so they divided Palestine, which was a province of the Ottoman Empire.
The League of Nations, after the First World War, divided it.
And part of it came under French rule.
And the other part came under British rule.
But it was officially not a colony.
It was a mandatory government.
They had-- they were supposed to lead it gradually to save government.
But they-- but they really governed it like a colony.
So the official government really was Britain.
But as far as the Jews were concerned, we had our own semi-official government that was-- that conducted so to see the Jewish affairs.
Yes?
We had the-- the immigration, and the development of the country, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the other organizations like that.
The unions-- there was a very powerful union, you know, where all workers belonged to, almost.
And so it was the whole economic and other life-- social life and everything-- was really run for the Jews by this, you know, semi-official government, called the Jewish Agency.
Yes.
Yeah.
Every kibbutz belonged automatically to the union.
Yeah.
And so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the Jewish community, the so-called, what's called [HEBREW] in Hebrew.
And it developed an economic life-- you know, there was private industry.
But the agriculture-- the agriculture was, to most part-- except the orange groves, they were privately owned.
But the other agriculture [INAUDIBLE] were most-- the most part-- were run by kibbutzim, or by cooperative villages, where the machinery was owned [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by-- what do you call?
Commonly owned, you know?
But the ground was owned or rented by individual families.
All the-- all the land was-- was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was bought by a Jewish organization.
It was called the Jewish National Fund.
They bought land from Jewish money that was contributed, you know, they were Jews.
And then they leased that land for 99 years to a kibbutz, or to one of these cooperative villages.
And this was a mainstay of the agriculture in Palestine.
No.
But-- no-- the kibbutz, I was in the kibbutz.
Yeah?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I left the kibbutz.
Sponsor me?
What do you mean by sponsor me?
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I lived in the kibbutz.
And the kibbutz was-- is based on common-- on no individual property.
Even the clothes that we wore did not belong to us, you know, to the whole-- to the whole community.
So every week, we went to the store, to the-- to the store-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] you know the store where you buys things, I mean, where things are stored.
What do you call it in English?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The store, yeah?
And we got our clothes for the week.
And at the end of the week, we changed it.
Because it was already standing by itself from sweat.
[LAUGHS] So nothing really belonged to us.
And the money, we didn't get any money.
The money all went into one common cache.
So there's no private-- there was no private property, and no private money.
It has changed, I mean, very much.
Much more-- the kids, the children were brought up in children's houses.
They did not live with the parents.
We ate in a common dining hall, common kitchen.
There was an old-- there were no hired people working.
Everyone was a member of a kibbutz.
That means, after a time of probation, yes?
Then the community had to vote everybody in as a member.
And only members could work in or for the kibbutz.
It has changed very much now.
It's very different now.
No.
No.
No.

But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the difficulty was at the old kibbutz, the people that lived already for a year in the kibbutz or longer, or longer than the newcomers, in that kibbutz where I was, didn't know how to-- how to receive and integrate newcomers.
They're very bad in that.
And so I never felt as part of it.
And not only I-- at the time I was there, about 80 people left.
And I don't remember how many members there were.
There were two kibbutz movements, or three.
But the two main movements, where one was for larger kibbutz, and the other one was for small, limited kibbutz-- with limited people.
And the one with the limited, they were more political, and more to the left-- they were communists.
Most of them-- many of them were communist, really communist.
I mean, so the whole system actually was communistic, yes?
But politically, the larger kibbutz, we were more moderate, more social democrat-like.
Where the small ones were more leaning towards political communists.
Did you have any-- identify with any political groups?
Well, I sympathized with the communists.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Germany, we were social democrats.
But then-- then I gradually started to sympathize with communism.
No.
No.
Yeah.
We all-- we all-- my wife.
At that time, most people were immigrants.
Pardon?
My wife?
No.
No, not really.
I was alone.
After-- I met-- I met on the ship a few others.

And we-- and we-- there was a small group of about six people, I think, that all chose-- when we came, we were first brought into a camp, only one camp-- for new immigrants from this organization-- the-- the worker's organization.
See, there were-- people immigrated on a different-- on a different basis.
They came as individuals, and settled wherever they wanted to, and opened-- opened the workshops, or stores in the cities.
But my organization is Zionist Worker's Organization.
We went there with the intention of settling-- of mostly joining a kibbutz, yes.
So-- and we were-- we were given a choice which kibbutz we want to go to.
So I, unfortunately, chose that kibbutz.
If I wouldn't, I would have chosen another kibbutz.
I probably would still be in Israel today.
So this was a bad beginning, really.
One of those left-wing, small kibbutz-- smaller kibbutz.
And not too much because of the political orientation, but because of the more of the smaller community, more-- more-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] intimate community.
There-- these big kibbutzim, some of them had 1,000 people as members.
So there was no, really, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] common social life, you know.
Huh?
After I left?
I had a friend from town-- from my home town, from this family where the head of the family taught Hebrew.
He has a brother in Haifa.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so he-- and so I met him.
And we became good friends.
So when I wanted to leave the kibbutz, i sent to him, I think, a telegram, and he said he would find me a job.
And so-- and then he notified me that he had a job for me, and I could come.
That's when I left.
And the job was as a helper for-- of a-- of a house painter, painter who worked on new buildings.
[DRINKS]
So he just had a building, a new construction.
And that's where I worked, for, you know, painting.
And he paid me very little, 9 piastres a day, 5 piastres, 4 piastres cost a meal-- and he paid me 9 piastres.
And then he had another building coming, big building.
But the builder went bankrupt.
So he couldn't get that-- couldn't get that job.
So he said to me-- and that was in 1938, I think, '37.
He said-- that was during the Arab Uprising.
So it was really-- there were two hostile camps, the Arab areas and the Jewish areas.
And you took your life in your hands when you-- when you crossed over.
So he said, I can have a government job, for the British government, in the city of Akko.
It is at the Gulf of Haifa.
And was it-- he said, I can have a government-- government job there, painting the government buildings.
And-- and so, if you want to come, you have to take an Arab bus every morning and back to work-- to and from work.
